problem_statement: The document investigates how developers perceive the security of AI-generated code from GenAI tools like Copilot and ChatGPT, since these tools may reproduce vulnerable code learned from open-source training data.
magnitude_evidence: studies have shown that Copilot generates insecure code about 40% of the time [31], and only 5 out of 21 programs produced by ChatGPT were initially secure [20]; the 2024 OSSRA report revealed 84% of analyzed codebases contain at least one vulnerability, with 74% having high-risk vulnerabilities
insufficiency_evidence: participants who had access to an AI assistant tended to write less secure code compared to those without [32]; only about a quarter of developers expressed confidence in AI-generated code; 43% expressed a lack of confidence in the security of AI-generated code
solution_mechanisms: code scanning tools for vulnerability detection
avoid submitting sensitive data to GAI tools
key_constructs: overreliance on AI without human oversight
Dunning-Kruger effect in secure coding self-assessment
